title: Mucosal Atomising Device (MAD)
content_markdown:
#### Indications

- Administration of a medication via nasal mucosa.

#### Contraindications

- Severe facial trauma.
- Suspected nasal fractures.
- Blood, excessive mucus, or obstruction in the nasal passage.

#### Precautions

- Age <2yo
The soft “cone” at the top of the device limits the use on smaller patients. For these patients, the cone may be carefully removed. This increases the chance of trauma to the nares by bumping with plastic, so extra care must be taken.
- Conditions which incompletely obstruct the nasal mucosa, or reduce perfusion to the area. 

Effects of medication may be less pronounced than expected due to reduced absorption via the nasal mucosa caused by:

- Recent use of nasal vasoconstrictors (e.g. cocaine, oxymetazoline)

- Upper respiratory tract infection

- Epistaxis

- Patients dependent on high flow nasal oxygen or non-invasive ventilator support.

#### Complications

- Mild, transient nasal discomfort.
- May precipitate coughing or sneezing in some patients.

#### Equipment Required

**Essential:**

- Intranasal Mucosal Atomization Device (MAD)
- 1ml Luer lock syringe- Required medication- Drawing-up needle or vial-access cannula
**Optional:**

- Tissues
- Adequately sized Luer lock “reservoir” syringe to hold entire volume of medication ampoule.
- Inter-syringe connector (ISC)
#### Drawing from glass ampoule

- Perform hand hygiene, don clean gloves. 

- Draw entire contents of ampoule into an adequately sized Luer lock “reservoir” syringe

- Attach ISC to “reservoir” syringe and attached 1mL syringe to other side of ISC.

- Draw half the dose into the 1mL syringe from the “reservoir” syringe.

- Remove the 1mL syringe from the ISC

#### Drawing from vial/plastic amp

- Perform hand hygiene, don clean gloves.

- Draw half the desired dose into the 1mL syringe.

#### Attaching the MAD

- Attach the MAD device securely to the 1mL syringe (Fig. 1) 

Figure 1

#### Administration

- Position patient upright with head slightly back or reclining at 45\xb0.

- Inspect nostrils for obstructions; clear if necessary.

- Place MAD tip snugly against the nostril, aiming slightly up and outward (towards the tip of the ear) (Fig. 2) 

Figure 2

- Administer entire content of the 1mL syringe into the nostril. (Fig. 3) by briskly compressing the plunger.
The ideal volume is up to 0.3mL per nare, the maximum of 1mL per nare is supported. 

Figure 3

- Remove the MAD from the 1mL syringe and repeat steps 3-5 (glass amp.) or step 7 (vial/plastic amp.)

- Repeat steps 9 – 11, ensuring the opposite nostril is used (Fig. 4) 

Figure 4

- Store the 1mL syringe with the MAD still attached with the MAD sheltered within the MAD packaging.

- Perform hand hygiene.

#### Notes

- The device is latex-free and typically for single-patient use but can be re-used by the same patient during the same episode of care after rinsing and air-drying.

##### Dead Space

- When attached to a 1mL Luer lock syringe, the MAD300 has a “dead space” of 0.1mL
- The dead space is the volume of medication that remains in the device after actuation.
- There is no requirement to draw up extra volume to “prime” the device, unless explicitly instructed in the Clinical Pharmacology Pages or the Medication Calculator.
##### For maximum efficacy

- **Minimize volume, maximize concentration**
- The nasal cavity has a low volume capacity (approximately 100-150 \xb5L).
- Use the appropriately concentrated drug as per CMG
- **Maximize total mucosal absorptive surface area**
- Press briskly, rather than “dripping” the medication – Atomization produces particles 30-100 microns, maximizing surface area coverage. 
- Use BOTH nostrils to double the absorptive surface area – even when dosage could fit in one nostril. - Aim slightly up and outwards to cover the turbinates and olfactory mucosa- **Prepare the patient**
- Instruct the patient that they do not need to inhale during medication administration.
- Positioned patient upright with their head tilted slightly back, or reclining at a 45-degree angle.

#### Governance Details

##### PPC Level

 **Contact**  **Droplet**  **Airborne**  **Other** 
 \u2714    

##### Applicable Scope(s) of Practice

 **Non-Clinical Staff**  **NEPT**  **NEPT Para. Part.**  **Ambulance Paramedic**  **Intensive Care Paramedic**  **Other** 
   \u2714  \u2714  \u2714  

##### Applicable Specialist Skillsets

 **Flight Intensive Care Paramedic**  **Extended Care Paramedic**  **Remote Area Paramedic**  **Complex Patient Vehicle Operator**  **Other** 
 \xa0

##### Type
A naturally occurring gas 

##### Presentations
Compressed gas in all-white cylinders of various sizes, to be fitted with an appropriate pressure-reducing device 

##### Actions
Essential element for aerobic metabolic needs and sustaining life. 
 Oxygen administration improves, or prevents a reduction in, the oxygen content of blood leaving the lungs. 

#### Uses
A wide range of situations, for the treatment or prevention of hypoxaemia

#### Adverse Effects

- Increased risk of fire / explosion – oxygen strongly supports combustion 

- Mucosal dryness and irritation

- Hypoventilation in some COPD patients with CO2 retention (titrate oxygen to maintain SpO2 88 – 92%)

#### Contraindications

- Known paraquat poisoning with SpO2 ≥88%

- History of bleomycin therapy with SpO2 ≥88%

#### Precautions
Neonates (especially pre-term infants) are particularly susceptible to the toxic effects of oxygen. When absolutely required, the lowest effective concentration, for the shortest possible time, should be used to achieve adequate oxygenation.

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Safe to use

##### Breastfeeding
- Safe to use

#### Doses##### Adult and Paediatric
With the exception of conditions listed below, oxygen should generally be titrated to maintain SpO2 ≥94% (i.e, unless otherwise required, there is no need to push saturations to 100%). 

Deliver oxygen through a device appropriate to the required inspired concentration of oxygen.

- **Nasal cannulae: **1 – 4 litres/min (approx. 24 – 40% oxygen) for low oxygen requirements
- **Oxygen (‘Hudson’) mask: **6 – 15 litres/min (approx. 30 – 65% oxygen) for moderate oxygen requirements
- **Nebuliser: **8 litres/min for moderate oxygen requirements with need for aerosolised medications- **Non-rebreather mask (NRBM): **15 litres/min (approx. 60 – 97% oxygen) for high oxygen requirements- **Bag-valve-mask (BVM): **15 litres/min or demand valve (up to 100% oxygen) for high oxygen requirements- **Continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP): **8 – 15 litres/min (titrated to achieve desired effect/pressure) for high oxygen requirements.
#### Conditions with specific oxygen requirement
- Conditions requiring 100% oxygen delivery (NRBM, BVM) 
- Obstetric emergencies
- Carbon monoxide poisoning
- Unrelieved upper airway obstruction
- Diving emergencies.
- COPD patients with possible CO2 retention – titrate oxygen delivery to maintain SpO2 of 88 – 92%.
- Rapid sequence intubation (RSI) – aim for highest SpO2 possible 
- High flow oxygen (15 litres/min) via nasal cannulae is mandatory during this procedure, in addition to IPPV.

#### Indications
- For patients that require supplemental oxygen.

#### Procedure

Figure 1

##### Nasal Prongs (minimal oxygen requirements – approx 24-40%)

- Attach nasal prong tubing to the oxygen port.

- Select oxygen flow rate. 
- **Note: 15 Lpm continuous during intubation.**

- Place one nasal prong in each nostril and tubing tucked behind each ear.

- Position the slide adjuster underneath the patient’s chin.

- Reassess respiratory and perfusion status.

Figure 2

##### Hudson Mask (moderate oxygen requirements – approx 30-65%)

- Attach the oxygen tubing to the Hudson mask.

- Connect the oxygen tubing to the oxygen port.

- Place elastic banding over patients head and adjust as required.

- Position the mask over the patient’s nose and mouth, moulding the metal nose piece if required.

- Reassess respiratory and perfusion status.

Figure 3

##### Non-Rebreather Mask (high oxygen requirements - approx 60-97%)

- Prepare as for Hudson mask.

- Select oxygen flow rate of >10 Lpm.

- Ensure reservoir bag is inflated.

- Place elastic banding over patients head and adjust as required.

- Position the mask over the patient’s nose and mouth, moulding the nose piece if required.

- Reassess respiratory and perfusion status.
